findings.title: Hardcoded test credentials in source data files
findings.evidence: User credentials stored in plain text: code '20235218' with password 'ulima123', code '20232548' with password 'ulima123' and other test user entries with plaintext passwords.
findings.impact: Student login credentials are committed to version control and distributed with the app binary. This allows any user with access to the app assets to obtain valid authentication credentials for any hardcoded test user account.
findings.remediation: Remove all hardcoded user credentials from assets/data/users.json. If test data is needed, either (1) exclude this file from version control via .gitignore, (2) populate test data at runtime from a secure test API backend, or (3) use separate test fixtures that are not included in production builds.
findings.verifyNotes: The issue is REAL but severity was OVERSTATED. Hardcoded test credentials do exist in assets/data/users.json with plaintext passwords (9 accounts, all password 'ulima123'). However, these JSON files are NOT registered in pubspec.yaml assets, so they are NOT included in compiled app binaries and NOT accessible to app users. The credentials are stored in source code/repository which is the actual risk. No code in the app loads or uses this file for authentication - all auth goes through a backend API. The severity is MEDIUM (not CRITICAL) because: (1) files aren't in app distribution, (2) authentication is backend-based, but (3) real student credentials appear exposed in source repository which needs to be addressed if repo is shared or public.

findings.title: Insecure HTTP fallback for API endpoints on web and localhost
findings.evidence: api_client.dart uses HTTP protocol by default: 'http://localhost:3000' for web, 'http://10.0.2.2:3000' for Android emulator, and 'http://localhost:3000' for iOS. The baseUrl property constructs URIs without enforcing HTTPS.
findings.impact: Tokens, passwords, and user data are transmitted in cleartext over HTTP in dev/test builds. Man-in-the-middle attacks can intercept authentication tokens and session credentials. This is acceptable only for truly local development but is dangerous if these defaults are used against remote backends.
findings.remediation: Enforce HTTPS in production builds. For development: (1) Use API_BASE_URL build environment variable to override baseUrl with secure backend URL, (2) Add certificate pinning or HttpClient configuration to validate HTTPS, (3) Add compile-time assertions to prevent HTTP-only builds from connecting to production APIs.
findings.verifyNotes: The insecure HTTP defaults are confirmed in api_client.dart lines 30-34. However, the code implements a proper override mechanism via the API_BASE_URL environment variable (lines 24-28) using String.fromEnvironment(). Developers can set HTTPS endpoints during build with: flutter run --dart-define=API_BASE_URL=https://... The defaults are appropriate for local development, but MUST be overridden for any production or remote backend deployment. The severity is medium (not high) because the mitigation mechanism is properly implemented and available—the risk only manifests if developers fail to override the defaults when deploying to production. This is a configuration/documentation issue rather than a code defect."

findings.title: Excessive debug logging of application state and errors
findings.evidence: 15+ instances of print() and debugPrint() statements logging student IDs, error messages, course data, and application state. Example: 'print("✓ Notas guardadas para estudiante: $idEstudiante")' exposes student identifiers; 'print("✓ Datos de cursos cargados: ${_coursesData.length} cursos")' logs data counts.
findings.impact: Debug logs are visible in app console/logcat and may be captured in crash reporting or device logs. In a compromised or user-rooted device, these logs could expose student IDs, enrollment data, course information, and error details that aid attackers in understanding system structure.
findings.remediation: Remove all debug print() statements from production code. For logging: (1) Use a logger package with log-level filtering (e.g., logger package), (2) Strip debug logs in release builds via code transforms or conditional compilation, (3) Only log sanitized error messages without sensitive data (e.g., log 'Error saving data' not 'Error saving data for student 12345').
findings.verifyNotes: medium/low severity — accepted without adversarial verification

findings.title: Plaintext storage of student code in SharedPreferences
findings.evidence: Student code (username/identifier) is stored in SharedPreferences: 'Future<void> saveCode(String code) => _prefs.setString(_kCode, code);'. SharedPreferences provides no encryption on Android (stored in XML plaintext) or iOS (stored in standard user defaults).
findings.impact: Student code is stored unencrypted in device storage. If a device is compromised or stolen, attackers can easily extract student identifiers and use them with other attacks (credential stuffing, account enumeration). On Android, any app with READ_EXTERNAL_STORAGE or direct file access can read SharedPreferences.
findings.remediation: Migrate student code storage to FlutterSecureStorage (already used for tokens): change 'saveCode()' to use '_secureStorage.write()' instead of '_prefs.setString()'. FlutterSecureStorage encrypts data at rest using platform keystore (Android Keystore, iOS Keychain).
findings.verifyNotes: medium/low severity — accepted without adversarial verification

findings.title: Student setup data persisted in plaintext SharedPreferences
findings.evidence: Career ID, specialty selections, and setup completion status stored in plaintext: '_prefs.setInt(_kCareerId, careerId)', '_prefs.setString(_kEspecialidades, jsonEncode(...))', '_prefs.setBool(_kSetupComplete, setupComplete)'. Also persists per-user setups in '_kUserSetups' as JSON-encoded strings in SharedPreferences.
findings.impact: Student academic profile (career, specialties, enrollment status) is stored unencrypted. Combined with plaintext student code, this enables full profile reconstruction of any student on a compromised device.
findings.remediation: Move all setup data to FlutterSecureStorage or use encrypted SharedPreferences. Alternatively, fetch setup data fresh from backend on app launch instead of caching in local storage.
findings.verifyNotes: medium/low severity — accepted without adversarial verification

findings.title: Missing .gitignore entries for sensitive configuration files
findings.evidence: .gitignore does not include '.env', 'local.properties', 'GoogleService-Info.plist', or other common Firebase/OAuth configuration files. The file only ignores build artifacts but not configuration secrets.
findings.impact: If developers accidentally commit .env files, firebase config, or API keys in the future, these will not be automatically ignored by git. This increases risk of credential exposure in commits.
findings.remediation: Add the following lines to .gitignore: '.env', '.env.*', 'local.properties', 'GoogleService-Info.plist', 'google-services.json', '*.keystore', 'key.properties', '.firebase/'
findings.verifyNotes: medium/low severity — accepted without adversarial verification

findings.title: Loose dependency constraints on production packages
findings.evidence: url_launcher: ^6.3.2, get: ^4.6.5, http: ^1.6.0, shared_preferences: ^2.3.4, google_sign_in: ^6.2.1, flutter_svg: ^2.0.10+1 - All use caret constraints allowing major version changes
findings.impact: Dependency updates can introduce breaking changes. google_sign_in has upgrades available (6.3.0 → 7.2.0) and transitive deps have version gaps (e.g., google_sign_in_android 6.2.1 → 7.2.13). This risks unexpected behavior in production.
findings.remediation: Pin critical dependencies to specific versions: e.g., google_sign_in: '6.3.0', url_launcher: '6.3.2', http: '1.6.0'. Use ~X.Y.Z for patch-only updates if updates are desired.
findings.verifyNotes: The claim is based on real observations: pubspec.yaml does use caret constraints (^) on production packages at the specified lines. However, the severity is overstated. Caret constraints are the Dart/Flutter standard and actually prevent major version changes (e.g., ^6.2.1 will not allow 7.x). The real risk is limited to minor/patch updates within the locked major version. The claim's example about google_sign_in upgrading from 6.3.0 to 7.2.0 is misleading—7.x would not be selected by ^6.2.1. Some transitive dependencies are outdated (e.g., google_sign_in_android 6.2.1), creating a moderate maintenance burden. The issue exists but should be classified as medium risk rather than high, as this is standard dependency management practice in Dart projects.

findings.title: Outdated transitive dependencies with major version gaps
findings.evidence: google_sign_in 6.3.0 (latest 7.2.0), google_sign_in_android 6.2.1 (latest 7.2.13), google_sign_in_ios 5.9.0 (latest 6.3.0), vector_graphics 1.2.1 (latest 1.2.2), vector_graphics_compiler 1.2.2 (latest 1.2.5)
findings.impact: Significant lag in platform-specific packages (google_sign_in) creates compatibility risks and misses security patches. vector_graphics updates may include important fixes.
findings.remediation: Run 'flutter pub upgrade' to pull in compatible upgrades. For google_sign_in, consider upgrading to 7.2.0+ if compatible with your code.
findings.verifyNotes: The claim accurately identifies the dependency versions in pubspec.lock: google_sign_in 6.3.0, google_sign_in_android 6.2.1, google_sign_in_ios 5.9.0, vector_graphics 1.2.1, and vector_graphics_compiler 1.2.2 at the cited lines. However, the severity is MEDIUM rather than HIGH because: (1) google_sign_in's constraint in pubspec.yaml is ^6.2.1, which intentionally caps at major version 6, suggesting deliberate version pinning rather than neglect; (2) transitive dependencies like platform (3.1.6) are not directly controllable; (3) vector_graphics gap (1.2.1 to claimed 1.2.2) is a minor patch version and low-risk. The real issue is the unclaimed constraint on google_sign_in allowing a major version 6 when versions 7+ exist, but this reflects the project's declared dependency policy, not lock file drift. Not a critical debt item without evidence the team wants to upgrade to 7.x."

findings.title: Dead code unreachable due to early return
findings.evidence: Line 94 has 'return e.message;' followed by unreachable line 95 'return 'No se pudo conectar con el servidor.';' in the same catch block
findings.impact: Dead code indicates logic error. The fallback error message will never execute, potentially masking genuine connection failures.
findings.remediation: Remove the unreachable return statement or restructure the catch logic to handle different exception types appropriately.
findings.verifyNotes: Confirmed genuine dead code: line 95 'return 'No se pudo conectar con el servidor.';' is unreachable because line 94 has 'return e.message;' in the same catch block. This is a logic error where an early return prevents fallback error handling. The severity is MEDIUM (not high) because: (1) the current behavior of returning e.message is functionally safe and won't crash; (2) however, it does lose the defensive fallback intent, and if e.message is null/empty, no proper error message would be returned. The correct fix would be: 'return e.message ?? 'No se pudo conectar con el servidor.';' Categorized as error-handling debt."

findings.title: Pervasive use of print() in production code
findings.evidence: 18+ instances of print() calls across services and controllers. flutter analyze reports 'avoid_print' violations in 8+ files
findings.impact: Debug output clutters production logs, impacts app performance, and exposes internal state. Makes troubleshooting harder by mixing debug and user-facing output.
findings.remediation: Replace all print() calls with a structured logging framework (e.g., logger, talker, or custom wrapper). Disable debug output in release builds.
findings.verifyNotes: CONFIRMED: There are exactly 21 print() statements across 7 Dart files (not 18+ as claimed, but the claim is accurate overall). Files verified: nota_tile.dart (line 81), app_header.dart (line 37), curso_model.dart (line 33), calculadora_controller.dart (lines 36, 38, 91-93, 95, 147), courses_service.dart (lines 32, 34), evaluations_service.dart (lines 43, 45), notas_service.dart (lines 33, 35, 45, 52, 61, 63, 72, 82). All print statements are informational/diagnostic with emoji prefixes (✓, ✗, ⚠️, ✅). The issue is REAL but severity should be MEDIUM, not HIGH. Reasoning: (1) Print statements are contextually used in try-catch blocks and initialization/loading methods—they're intentional logging, not accidental debug code; (2) They don't expose sensitive data—only counts and generic status messages; (3) The impact on production logs is manageable if the app controls where logs go (Flutter apps can redirect stdout); (4) This is a code-quality issue requiring refactoring to use a proper logging framework, but it's not a critical security or performance concern. Better classification: technical debt requiring migration from print() to a structured logging solution (e.g., logger package, Sentry, Firebase Crashlytics), but not high-severity in isolation.

findings.title: Deprecated Color.withOpacity() used extensively

findings.impact: Deprecated API usage risks breaking in future Flutter versions. Precision loss in color transparency values can cause visual inconsistencies.
findings.remediation: Replace Color.withOpacity(x) with Color.withValues(alpha: x) throughout the codebase. This preserves color precision.

findings.title: Missing error states and loading states in key pages
findings.evidence: Pages like DescripCursos have `isLoading` RxBool but no corresponding error state display. malla_page shows loading spinner (line 35-40) but no error fallback UI. calculadora_page has no loading state at all for the initial data load.

findings.title: Race condition: CoursesService and EvaluationSyllabusService called before load() in main.dart
findings.evidence: main() calls `Future.wait([...loadEvaluationData(), ...loadCoursesData()])` (line 30-32) but then immediately creates pages that access CoursesService.allCourses and EvaluationSyllabusService.allSyllabuses. If the Future.wait completes but assignment is still pending, accessing the late fields will crash.
findings.impact: Rare but critical: late-init crash if the async load hasn't completed before the page tries to access the data.
findings.remediation: Ensure Future.wait completes fully before accessing the services. Or make allCourses/allSyllabuses return empty list safely until loaded. Use `_isLoaded` flag more strictly.

findings.title: Row type casting with 'as unknown as' pattern everywhere
findings.evidence: Across 38+ type assertions using 'as unknown as Array<...>' pattern. While this works, it's verbose and indicates implicit typing. TypeScript best practice is to type the execute() call result directly.

findings.remediation: If db.execute() is generic, consider wrapping: `const query = <T>(sql: SQL) => db.execute(sql) as Promise<T[]>;` Then use: `const rows = await query<ProfileRow>(sql...)`. Or ask Drizzle to provide typed queries directly.

findings.title: Inconsistent field naming conventions in response objects

findings.title: skipLibCheck enabled, masking potential type issues
findings.evidence: tsconfig.json sets 'skipLibCheck': true, which skips type-checking of .d.ts files in node_modules. This is often done to speed up TypeScript but can hide version mismatches or incompatibilities between declared types and actual runtime behavior, especially important given the loose dependency versions and unused deps (google-auth-library, AWS SDK).
findings.impact: Type errors in third-party libraries (or in our type assertions) go undetected; false confidence in type safety; harder to debug runtime type errors.
findings.remediation: Change 'skipLibCheck' to false (or remove it, default is false in strict mode). If compilation becomes slow, profile and optimize specific slow packages rather than blanket-skipping. Alternatively, keep it but add a pre-commit 'type-check' step with skipLibCheck disabled.
findings.verifyNotes: medium/low severity — accepted without adversarial verification

findings.title: No .env file validation or documentation
findings.evidence: .env file is committed to git (likely contains sensitive values like DATABASE_URL and AWS/Google credentials based on .env.example). .env should never be committed; only .env.example with placeholders should be. test-db.cjs and patch-schedule-classrooms.cjs hardcode DATABASE_URL in scripts.
findings.impact: Security risk: database credentials and API keys exposed in git history; accidental secret leak to public repos; difficult to rotate credentials; CI/CD secrets management confusion.
findings.remediation: 1) Add .env to .gitignore immediately. 2) Run 'git rm --cached .env' and 'git commit'. 3) Rotate any exposed secrets. 4) Keep only .env.example with placeholder values. 5) Document required env vars in README or .env.example with comments.

findings.title: No pagination on list endpoints

findings.title: Race condition in token version validation

findings.title: Unverified lint rules in analysis_options.yaml - linter is nearly unconfigured

findings.title: Loose type safety with Map<String, dynamic> used extensively
findings.evidence: cursos defined as <Map<String, dynamic>>[].obs; curso accessed as Map in loops; 'curso['notas']', 'curso['nombre']', 'curso['codigoSeccion']' - all string lookups without type safety; no model class wrapping the data
findings.impact: No compile-time type checking; runtime crashes on missing keys; poor IDE autocomplete; violates DRY and makes refactoring fragile
findings.remediation: Create Curso and NotaRecord model classes; use typed lists instead of Map<String, dynamic>; add fromJson/toJson for type-safe parsing

findings.title: Course status map stored unencrypted in SharedPreferences
findings.evidence: saveStatuses() stores course completion/enrollment status as JSON in plaintext SharedPreferences: 'await _prefs.setString(_kStatuses, jsonEncode(map))' and also persists per-user status maps in '_kUserStatuses'.
findings.impact: Student enrollment and course progress status is unencrypted. While lower sensitivity than credentials, this data combined with student ID can reveal course history and academic performance.
findings.remediation: Move status maps to FlutterSecureStorage alongside token and setup data. Use consistent encrypted storage for all personal student data.
findings.verifyNotes: medium/low severity — accepted without adversarial verification

findings.title: Google Sign-In without server-side validation of idToken
findings.evidence: loginWithGoogle() obtains idToken from Google Sign-In and sends it directly to backend via POST /auth/google without local validation. Client-side validation is not shown; the token is passed as-is to the backend.
findings.impact: While the token should be validated server-side (which is the responsibility of the backend /auth/google endpoint), the client should also verify the token locally for defense-in-depth. Missing local validation means a compromised backend validation could allow spoofed tokens.
findings.remediation: Add optional client-side validation of the Google idToken using the google_id_token package or similar before sending to backend. Validate token signature, expiration, and audience. This is a defense-in-depth measure; backend validation is the primary control.
findings.verifyNotes: medium/low severity — accepted without adversarial verification

findings.title: Podfile.lock and Podfile.lock committed (iOS dependencies)
findings.evidence: Both lock files are committed to git (verified via 'git ls-files'). These platform-specific lock files should be regenerated per environment.
findings.impact: CocoaPods lock files can cause build inconsistencies across developers if not regenerated. Increases repository size with binary metadata.
findings.remediation: Add 'ios/Podfile.lock' and 'macos/Podfile.lock' to .gitignore. Ensure iOS developers run 'flutter pub get' to regenerate locally.

findings.title: Release build version pinned to 1.0.0+1
findings.evidence: version: 1.0.0+1 in pubspec.yaml. flutter.versionName=1.0.0, flutter.versionCode=1 in local.properties (hardcoded).
findings.impact: Version must be manually updated for each release. No automated versioning. Makes it difficult to track deployed versions.
findings.remediation: Implement a versioning strategy (semantic versioning + build metadata). Use CI/CD to auto-increment build codes or adopt a tool like pubspec_extract_version.

findings.title: Unreachable code in auth_service.login() — line 95 is dead
findings.evidence: In the ApiException catch block (line 87), line 94 returns `e.message`. Line 95 `return 'No se pudo conectar con el servidor.';` is unreachable dead code.
findings.impact: Dead code is a sign of incomplete error handling logic. The fallback message is never used, suggesting the error path may be incomplete or the logic was refactored incorrectly.
findings.remediation: Remove line 95 or refactor the catch block to have a generic catch clause for non-ApiException errors: `} on ApiException catch (e) { ... } catch (_) { return 'No se pudo conectar con el servidor.'; }`
findings.verifyNotes: The claim is accurate: line 95 is dead code unreachable due to the return statement on line 94. However, severity is overstated. This is a code quality/maintenance issue, not a functional bug. The error handling works correctly — it returns e.message instead of using the fallback. The issue appears to be incomplete refactoring (either the line 94 return should not exist, or line 95 should have different logic). Since the app functions correctly despite this dead code, severity should be 'low', not 'high'.

findings.title: No logout error handling — network errors silently ignored
findings.evidence: logout() method (line 199-206) calls `_api.postJson('/auth/logout', ...)` in a try-catch but the catch silently ignores all errors with comment 'Logout es stateless'. While conceptually correct, it means if logout() is called but network is down, the API call hangs and silently times out, blocking the logout flow.
findings.impact: Logout may appear to succeed locally but server session isn't terminated. Low severity because local session is cleared regardless, but server-side session orphaning can occur.
findings.remediation: Add timeout to logout request, or add logging to track silent errors. Ensure logout completes within reasonable time even if server is unreachable.
findings.verifyNotes: medium/low severity — accepted without adversarial verification
